title: Sen Operators and Lie Algebras Arising From Galois Representations Over p-adic Varieties
speaker: Tongmu He

proof_stages.label: Local Sen descent and classical operators
proof_stages.summary: For a complete discretely valued p-adic field with imperfect residue field, the speaker reviews Sen--Brinon--Tsuji descent from G_K-representations over completed algebraic closures to locally analytic representations of a simpler Galois group Gamma, whose Lie algebra gives arithmetic and geometric Sen operators.

proof_stages.label: Local inertia detection theorem
proof_stages.summary: The local theorem states that, for a Q_p-representation, the Lie algebra of the inertia image is exactly the smallest Q_p-subspace whose scalar extension contains all Sen operators, with vanishing operators equivalent to finite inertia image.

proof_stages.label: Coordinate-free local replacement by the dual Faltings extension
proof_stages.summary: Because the imperfect-residue Sen operators depend on choices of p-basis liftings and hence do not glue, the dual Faltings extension is introduced as a canonical Lie algebra, with a formula using generalized coordinates and a p-adic Simpson computation proving independence of all choices.

proof_stages.label: Affine semistable arithmetic setup and main construction theorem
proof_stages.summary: For a normal domain finite over a semistable chart, the arithmetic fundamental group and the relative Faltings extension are set up, and the main affine theorem constructs a canonical G-equivariant, functorial Lie algebra homomorphism from the dual Faltings extension to endomorphisms of any finite projective representation.

proof_stages.label: Descent and height-one gluing proof
proof_stages.summary: The proof combines Tsuji-style descent to a simpler tower, a noncanonical coordinate formula, and localization at height-one primes of the special fiber, where injectivity into products of completed DVRs gives uniqueness and compatibility with the already-proved local-field construction gives existence.

proof_stages.label: Inertia and quotient Lie algebra consequences
proof_stages.summary: The local inertia theorem is generalized by summing the Lie algebras of inertia images at all height-one special-fiber primes, yielding the corresponding minimality statement, the zero-operator criterion, and a universal map from the dual Faltings extension to Lie algebras of p-adic analytic quotients.

proof_stages.label: Global sheafification on the generically étale/Faltings site
proof_stages.summary: To globalize beyond affine semistable charts, the speaker introduces the generically étale site, uses semistable affines as a topological basis, interprets continuous representations as vector bundles on profinite étale/Faltings sites, and states the sheafified global Sen-operator construction.

examples.name: Vanishing Sen operators and finite inertia
examples.note: The special case in which all Sen operators vanish is presented to make the inertia theorem concrete: it is equivalent to finite inertia image, locally and then height-one-locally over the variety.
examples.engagement_reason: the vanishing-Sen-operators/finite-inertia case is stated as a special equivalence but not derived or checked in an example

passes.reason: The case of all Sen operators vanishing is a trivial infinitesimal-action case illustrating the finite-inertia criterion.

The zero Sen-operator case is a collapsed/trivial limiting case illustrating the finite-inertia criterion.
The all-zero Sen-operator case is a trivial/vanishing special case used to explain the inertia theorem.

examples.name: Two-component semistable curve with coordinate x
examples.note: The curve with components x=0 and y=0 shows that a coordinate can be good at one generic point but not another, motivating the enlargement to generalized coordinate systems and then to the Faltings-extension formulation.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the semistable two-component curve by tracking the coordinate x at the two generic points and checking where it is or is not a good p-basis coordinate

passes.reason: The reducible semistable two-component curve is a nodal degeneration used to obstruct a naive global choice of good coordinates.
the two-component semistable degeneration is used as an obstruction to a single coordinate remaining good at all height-one localizations
The two-component semistable normal-crossing curve is a degeneration and is used to show the naive choice of one global good coordinate fails after localization.
The nodal two-component semistable curve is a degeneration and is used to show that a global good coordinate need not localize to good coordinates everywhere.
A nodal two-component semistable degeneration is used to show that a naive choice of global good coordinate fails after localization.

The reducible semistable two-component curve is a degeneration and is used to show that a single good coordinate need not localize well on all components.

examples.name: Crystalline representation and D_cris question
examples.note: In response to a question, crystalline representations are used to recall that the arithmetic Sen operator has Hodge-Tate weights as eigenvalues, while the geometric operator's relation to the connection is left open.
examples.engagement_reason: the crystalline-representation/D_cris situation appears only in a question and receives a general answer about arithmetic Sen operators and Hodge-Tate weights, without working through D_cris

passes.reason: A crystalline representation with its D_cris module is a standard p-adic Hodge-theoretic test case raised for comparison.
